44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73

107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.

166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.

173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```

102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).

149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)

154	These run automatically between phases:

156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	\begin{definition}
46	
47	\leanok
48	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
49	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
51	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
52	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
53	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
54	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
55	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
56	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
57	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
58	  charts. Suppose given:
59	  \begin{itemize}
60	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
61	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
62	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
63	      \[
64	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
65	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
66	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
67	      \]
68	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
69	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
70	  \end{itemize}
71	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
72	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
73	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
74	  \((g_{ij})\):
75	  \begin{enumerate}
76	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
77	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
78	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
79	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each triple overlap
80	      \(V_{ij} \cap V_{ik}\) one has
81	      \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij} = g_{ik}\), the two sides being compared after
82	      transport through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\)
83	      (\(t_{ij}\) and the overlap immersions \(f_{ij}, f_{jk}, f_{ik}\)) so that
84	      all three pullbacks land on the same sheaf -- the multiplicative,
85	      \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition.
86	  \end{enumerate}
87	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
88	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
89	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
90	  construction produces:
91	  \begin{enumerate}
92	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
93	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
94	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
95	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
96	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
97	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
98	  \end{enumerate}
99	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
100	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
101	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
102	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
103	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
104	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
105	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
106	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
107	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
108	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
109	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
110	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
111	
112	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
113	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
114	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
115	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
116	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
117	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
118	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
119	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
120	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
121	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
122	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
123	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
124	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
125	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
126	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
127	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
128	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
129	  built.
130	
131	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
132	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
133	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
134	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
135	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
136	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
137	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
138	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
139	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
140	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
141	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
142	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
143	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
144	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
145	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
146	
147	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
148	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
149	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
150	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
151	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
152	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
153	\end{definition}

155	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
156	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
157	
158	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
159	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
160	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
161	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
162	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
163	
164	\begin{definition}\leanok
165	[Global section of the unit module]
166	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
167	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
168	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
169	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
170	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
171	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
172	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
173	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
174	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
175	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
176	  global function.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\begin{definition}\leanok
180	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
181	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
183	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
184	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
185	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
186	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
187	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
188	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
189	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
190	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
191	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
192	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
193	\end{definition}
194	
195	\begin{definition}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
199	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
201	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
202	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
203	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
204	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
205	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
206	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
207	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
208	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
209	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
210	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
212	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
213	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
214	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
215	  \[
216	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
217	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
218	  \]
219	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
220	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
221	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
222	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
223	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
224	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
225	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
226	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
227	
228	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
229	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
230	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
231	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
232	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
233	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
234	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
235	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
236	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
237	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
238	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
239	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
240	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
241	  free sheaves'' primitive.
242	\end{definition}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}\leanok
245	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
246	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
248	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
249	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
250	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
255	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
256	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
257	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
258	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
259	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
260	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
261	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
262	  \[
263	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
264	  \]
265	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
266	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
267	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
268	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
269	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
270	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
271	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
272	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
273	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
274	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
275	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
276	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
277	\end{proof}

279	\begin{definition}
280	
281	\leanok
282	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
283	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
284	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
285	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
286	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
287	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
288	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
289	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
290	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
291	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
292	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
293	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
294	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
295	  %  by putting
296	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
297	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
298	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
299	  \[
300	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
301	  \]
302	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
303	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
304	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
305	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
306	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
307	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
308	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
309	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
310	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
311	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
312	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
313	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
314	\end{definition}
315	
316	\begin{definition}
317	
318	\leanok
319	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
320	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
321	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
322	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
323	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
324	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
325	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
326	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
327	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
328	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
329	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
330	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
331	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
332	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
333	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
334	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
335	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
336	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
337	  \[
338	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
339	  \]
340	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
341	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
342	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
343	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
344	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
345	  \[
346	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
347	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
348	  \]
349	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
350	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
351	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
352	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
353	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
354	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
355	\end{definition}

357	\section{The functor of points}
358	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
359	
360	\begin{definition}
361	
362	\leanok
363	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
364	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
366	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
367	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
368	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
369	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
370	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
371	  % "represents the
372	  %  contravariant functor
373	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
374	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
375	  %  set of all equivalence classes
376	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
377	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
378	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
379	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
380	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
381	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
382	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
383	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
384	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
386	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
387	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
388	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
389	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
